claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions. Let me start with the first one:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?** Please also let me know their ages and names.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I do. I have one child - Alex, who's 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Since Alex is 17 years old, I need to ask: **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

(This is important because Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 if they remain in approved education or training.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any approved education or training. They left school and are working full-time now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to clarify one more thing:

**How many hours per week is Alex working in their full-time job?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is working about 24 hours a week, maybe a bit more.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me review the situation:

- Alex is 17 years old
- Alex is not in approved education or training
- Alex is working 24 hours or more per week

According to the Child Benefit rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training.

Since Alex has left school, is not in approved education or training, and is working 24 hours or more per week, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Eligibility Decision: You are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

The reason is that Alex is 17 years old, working 24 hours or more per week, and is not in approved education or training. Under Child Benefit rules, payments stop when a child aged 16 or over starts paid work for 24 hours or more per week and is no longer in approved education or training.

If Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if they reduce their working hours below 24 hours per week or return to approved education or training), you may become eligible again and could make a new claim at that time.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old. While Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training, Alex is not in approved education or training. According to the rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex is working approximately 24 hours or more per week and is not in approved education or training, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (17 years old), who has left school and is working 24 hours or more per week without being in approved education or training. Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility ends when a child aged 16 or over works 24+ hours per week and is not in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old. While Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training, Alex is not in approved education or training. According to the rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex is working approximately 24 hours or more per week and is not in approved education or training, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (17 years old), who has left school and is working 24 hours or more per week without being in approved education or training. Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility ends when a child aged 16 or over works 24+ hours per week and is not in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
